questions.question: Define exhaustive search and explain why it is also called brute-force approach.
keyPoints: Exhaustive search systematically examines ALL possible candidates
Also called brute-force because it uses maximum computational effort without clever optimizations
Checks every candidate until solution is found or all candidates exhausted

questions.question: Analyze the time complexity of bubble sort and explain why it is considered a brute-force algorithm.
keyPoints: Worst-case: O(n²) with n(n-1)/2 comparisons
Best-case: O(n) when array is already sorted (with optimization)
Brute-force because it uses simple nested loops comparing adjacent elements
No divide-and-conquer or optimization strategy employed

questions.question: What is backtracking? How does it optimize exhaustive search while still guaranteeing optimal solution?
keyPoints: Backtracking abandons partial solution when it cannot lead to valid solution
Prunes search tree by not exploring infeasible branches
Still guarantees optimal solution by exploring all valid paths
Reduces average case complexity significantly

questions.question: Explain the Subset Sum problem and analyze its complexity using exhaustive search.
keyPoints: Given n items with weights, find subset that sums to target W
Generate all 2^n subsets and check each
Time complexity: O(n × 2^n) - O(2^n) subsets, O(n) to compute each sum
Space complexity: O(n) for recursion stack

questions.question: When is brute-force approach preferred over more sophisticated algorithms?
keyPoints: When problem size is small (n < 20-30 typically)
When simplicity and correctness are more important than efficiency
When no better algorithm exists for the problem
As baseline for comparing with optimized algorithms
When hardware can handle the computational cost

questions.question: What is the difference between exhaustive search and greedy algorithm? Give example.
keyPoints: Exhaustive: checks ALL possibilities, guarantees optimal solution, O(2^n) or O(n!)
Greedy: makes locally optimal choice, faster but may miss global optimum
Example: TSP - greedy picks nearest unvisited city (fast but suboptimal), exhaustive finds optimal (expensive)

questions.question: Explain the 0/1 Knapsack problem and describe how exhaustive search solves it. Analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Problem: Given n items with weights and values, maximize value within weight capacity W
Exhaustive approach: Check all 2^n subsets of items
For each subset: calculate total weight and total value, if weight ≤ W, consider it as valid solution
Keep track of maximum value found among valid subsets
Time complexity: O(n × 2^n) - generate 2^n subsets, compute sum for each in O(n)
Space complexity: O(n) for recursion/backtracking stack
Limitation: Only practical for small n (typically n ≤ 20-25)

questions.question: Discuss the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) and explain why exhaustive search is impractical for large instances. What alternatives exist?
keyPoints: TSP: Find shortest Hamiltonian cycle visiting all cities exactly once
Exhaustive: Check all n! permutations of cities
Time complexity: O(n!) - extremely expensive
For n=20: approximately 2.4 × 10^18 operations

Alternatives: (1) Greedy: nearest neighbor heuristic O(n²) but not optimal (2) Branch and Bound: prune using lower bounds (3) Approximation algorithms: within factor of optimal (4) Heuristics: simulated annealing, genetic algorithms (5) Dynamic programming: O(n² × 2^n) Held-Karp algorithm

questions.question: Explain branch and bound technique. How does it optimize exhaustive search for optimization problems? Give example.
keyPoints: Branch and bound partitions solution space into branches
Computes lower bound (minimization) or upper bound (maximization) for each branch
Prunes branch if bound cannot yield better solution than current best
Explores promising branches first using best-first search
Example - 0/1 Knapsack: If current partial solution has value X and remaining items max possible is Y, total ≤ X+Y. If X+Y ≤ best found, prune branch.
Reduces search space significantly while guaranteeing optimal solution
Used in: Integer programming, TSP, scheduling problems

questions.question: Write and explain the algorithm for generating all permutations using exhaustive search. Analyze its complexity.
keyPoints: Approach 1 - Recursive: Fix first element, generate permutations of remaining n-1 elements, then fix second element, repeat
Approach 2 - Iterative: Use markers/used array to track available elements
Algorithm: For each position, try each unused element, recurse for next position, backtrack
Time complexity: O(n × n!) - n! permutations, O(n) to process each
Space complexity: O(n) for recursion stack and current permutation
Proves exhaustive: generates ALL n! possible orderings
Used in: TSP, scheduling, combinatorial problems
